coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-based antifungal coating film for active packaging application of avocado
coredata.dc:creator: Ezati, Parya
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Molaei, Rahim
Rezaei, Zeinab
coredata.dc:description: This study investigated the applicability of carbon dots (CDs) as a functional filler for producing active food packaging films. CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose as the carbon source. The CDs were used to prepare chitosan/gelatin (Chi/Gel)-based composite films. The addition of CDs increased the UV blocking property of the film by 99% without significant change in mechanical properties, water vapor permeability (WVP), and water contact angle (WCA). In addition, the CDs-added composite films showed potent antioxidant activity. The composite film generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) and exhibits high antibacterial activity against pathogenic bacteria, showing 100% destruction of L. monocytogenes and E. coli. The composite film also exhibited high antifungal activity against mold (Aspergillus flavus and Colletotrichum orbiculare) and had negligible cytotoxicity to L929 cells even after long-term exposure of 72 h. The chitosan/gelatin/CDs-film coating effectively inhibited the growth of mold on the surface of avocados and extended the fruit shelf life by more than 14 days. The CDs are likely to be a value-added, low-cost, and safe, functional nanofiller for preparing functional films for active packaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Antioxidant
Antifungal
Chitosan
Coating
Avocado

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) grant funded by the Korean government ( MSIT ) (No. 2022R1A2B02001422 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographic images and SEM images of (b) surface and (c) cross-section of the chitosan/gelatin-based films.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of the chitosan/gelatin-based films.
Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA) of the chitosan/gelatin-based films.
(a) Absorption spectra of ABTS and DPPH solutions with varying CDs concentrations and (b) antioxidant activity of the chitosan/gelatin-based films determined by ABTS and DPPH methods.
Antimicrobial activity of the chitosan/gelatin-based films against Listeria monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, Aspergillus flavus, and Colletotrichum orbiculare by the agar well diffusion method.
Antimicrobial activity of the chitosan/gelatin-based films against Listeria monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, Aspergillus flavus, and Colletotrichum orbiculare.

(a) SEM images of Listeria monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, and Aspergillus flavus treated with the CDs-added chitosan/gelatin-based films. (b) ROS generation efficiency of the chitosan/gelatin-based films after a one-hour exposure to sodium fluorescein in alkaline conditions.
(a) Viability and (b) optical microscopy images of mouse fibroblast L929 cells after 72 h treatment with the CDs-added chitosan/gelatin-based films as calculated from MTT.
Appearance change of avocado coated with the chitosan/gelatin-based films during storage at 25 °C.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of CDs.
Apparent color and transmittance of the chitosan/gelatin-based films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as a mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanical properties of the chitosan/gelatin-based films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as a mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-based antifungal coating film for active packaging application of avocado

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Aquaculture, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Aquaculture, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Aquaculture, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigated the applicability of carbon dots (CDs) as a functional filler for producing active food packaging films. CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose as the carbon source. The CDs were used to prepare chitosan/gelatin (Chi/Gel)-based composite films. The addition of CDs increased the UV blocking property of the film by 99% without significant change in mechanical properties, water vapor permeability (WVP), and water contact angle (WCA). In addition, the CDs-added composite films showed potent antioxidant activity. The composite film generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) and exhibits high antibacterial activity against pathogenic bacteria, showing 100% destruction of L. monocytogenes and E. coli. The composite film also exhibited high antifungal activity against mold (Aspergillus flavus and Colletotrichum orbiculare) and had negligible cytotoxicity to L929 cells even after long-term exposure of 72 h. The chitosan/gelatin/CDs-film coating effectively inhibited the growth of mold on the surface of avocados and extended the fruit shelf life by more than 14 days. The CDs are likely to be a value-added, low-cost, and safe, functional nanofiller for preparing functional films for active packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The extraordinary activity of carbon dots (CDs) has attracted excessive curiosity about food safety and biomedical applications in the past decade. Several studies conducted so far suggest that the efficacy of CDs in biomedical applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, bio-labeling, and photodynamic therapy, which future approve their safety when it comes to human contact applications (Moradi et al., 2021). Besides biocompatibility and water solubility, CDs show beneficial properties such as adsorption interactions, controllable surface functional groups, and small size, making them attach to biological surfaces for antimicrobial purposes (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, Roy & Min et al., 2022; Roy et al., 2021). CDs are promising candidates for developing active food packaging films as quantum fillers because of their excellent antimicrobial properties, extraordinary antioxidant activity, low toxicity along with high compatibility with most biopolymers (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022; Ezati et al., 2021). CDs-incorporated composite films not only impart antioxidant and antimicrobial properties but will also improve the physicochemical properties such as mechanical, UV protection, and barrier properties due to the formation of strong interaction between CDs and polymer matrix through a hydrophilic bond (Kousheh et al., 2020; Min et al., 2022).

In food packaging, the search for application materials with antibacterial, antioxidant, UV protection, non-toxic, and biocompatibility ensures the reliable and efficient quality of packaged food during transportation and storage (Ezati et al., 2021). Various innovative packaging applications such as chemical treatment (Tzortzakis & Chrysargyris, 2017), modified atmosphere packaging (Priyadarshi et al., 2020), active packaging films (Aghdam et al., 2020; Ezati et al., 2022; Li et al., 2019), and coating (Davoodi et al., 2020; Ezati et al., 2021) have been used to ensure product safety and extend the shelf life of food. Among the various methods available for this purpose, active coatings are created by dipping the food directly into the film-forming solution or by spraying the film-forming solution onto the food surface (Ban et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Active coatings are primarily used to effectively reduce the microbial load on food surfaces and maintain the quality of fresh fruits and vegetables. Antimicrobial coatings can protect food from microbial contamination, the most common cause of food spoilage (Davoodi et al., 2020).

More recently, supply chain issues involving perishable commodities such as avocados require careful storage management (Kassim & Workneh, 2020). Avocado is a popular, nutritious, and delicious fruit with physicochemical properties, medicinal properties, and numerous health benefits. Health-seeking customers prefer avocados because of their bioactive ingredients such as fiber, antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals, which are important and beneficial nutrients for human health (Cho et al., 2021). However, from an economic and technical point of view, the high perishability of this fruit is a fundamental problem in marketing. The quality of avocados deteriorates rapidly during ripening, causing enormous fruit and economic losses and limiting the shelf life of the fruit after harvest (Arackal & Parameshwari, 2021). Therefore, proper post-harvest treatment must be implemented to maintain avocado quality throughout the supply chain. This is where active food packaging comes into play, including active antibacterial and antioxidant ingredients that protect food from microbial contamination and oxidation.

Various biodegradable polymers from natural sources, including carbohydrates and proteins, are used as solid matrices for edible and degradable active coating films (Jeya Jeevahan et al., 2020). Chitosan and gelatin are widely used among these carbohydrates and proteins in the food and pharmaceutical industries due to their flexibility, stabilizing, texturing, and emulsifying properties (Roy & Rhim, 2021; Yadav et al., 2020). They have been used alone or in combination to develop packaging film materials due to their excellent film formability, biocompatibility, biodegradability, high transparency, and low cost (Priyadarshi & Rhim, 2020; Roy & Rhim, 2021).

Since CDs are obtained from a safe organic source and exhibit strong functional properties, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and low toxicity, they are expected to be used for active packaging applications (Lim et al., 2015). Therefore, the main objective of this study was to explore the potential of CDs as functional antioxidant and antimicrobial materials for active packaging applications. To this end, Chi/Gel-based functional coating films were prepared by incorporating CDs, and physicochemical and functional properties of the film were evaluated. In addition, the efficacy of the functional film was tested through a coating of avocados.

CDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method using an aqueous glucose solution (Cheng et al., 2019). As shown schematically in Scheme 1, for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs), 1 g of glucose was dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water with stirring and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined stainless steel reactor (100 mL) followed by the reactor was heated to 200 °C for 6 h in an oil bath. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the unreacted material. The supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, pore size: 0.22 µm, Germany). After that, the filtrate was freeze-dried to obtain a black powder of CDs and stored in a refrigerator before further testing. The prepared CDs were characterized using the methods described in the Supporting Information.

Chitosan/gelatin-based films were prepared using a solution casting method. For this, 2 g each of chitosan and gelatin and 1.2 g of glycerol were dissolved in 150 mL of distilled water with vigorous stirring at 80 °C for 30 min. Afterward, 0.04 and 0.08 g of CDs (1 and 2 wt% based on polymer) was added slowly to the solution with vigorous stirring for one hour. The film-forming solution was promptly cast onto a leveled Teflon film (Cole-Parmer Instrument Co., Chicago, IL, USA) coated glass plate (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried at 25 °C for two days. Before further testing, the dried film was peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50% RH for at least two days. In addition, a neat Chi/Gel film was prepared using the same procedure without CD addition. The prepared films were designated Chi/Gel (chitosan/gelatin), Chi/Gel/CD1 (containing 1 wt% CDs), and Chi/Gel/CD2 (containing 2 wt% CDs). The details of the biocomposite film characterization process are provided in Supporting Information.

Antioxidant activities of the films were assessed using DPPH• and ABTS•+ radical scavenging methods. For DPPH analysis, a methanolic solution of DPPH was prepared, and ~100 mg of the film sample was added to a 20 mL DPPH solution, incubated for 360 min at room temperature, measured the absorbance at 517 nm. For ABTS analysis, potassium sulfate (2.45 mM) was mixed with ABTS solution (7 mM) and incubated overnight in the dark to make an ABTS assay solution. Hundred mg of film samples were added to 20 mL of ABTS assay solution and incubated at room temperature for 30 min in the dark, and then absorbance was measured at 734 nm. The free radical scavenging activity was calculated as follows:
(1)
Free radical scavenging activity
%
=
A
c
−
A
s
A
c
× 100
where Ac and As were the absorbance of ABTS or DPPH solutions of the control and test solutions.

The antimicrobial activity of the films was investigated by the agar well diffusion method against Listeria monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, Aspergillus flavus, and Colletotrichum orbiculare in solid media. An inoculum of 6 log CFU/mL was spread on the surface of agar plates using a sterilized swab. After that, wells with a diameter of 8 mm were punched on agar, filled with 100 μL of each film solution, and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h for bacteria and 3 days for fungus, then measured the diameter of the inhibition zone (Kousheh et al., 2020; Razavi et al., 2020).

The films' antimicrobial activity was also evaluated using a total viable colony count method against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, A. flavus, and C. orbiculare following Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, Roy, and Min et al. (2022).

Avocado fruits were used to test the films' efficacy in assessing shelf life fruit. The fruits were directly immersed in the film solution for 1 min and dried at room temperature. The uncoated control and coated fruits with a thin film, either the neat Chi/Gel film or CDs-incorporated films, were put in a tray and stored at 25 °C for 21 days. A digital camera recorded the visual changes of each group at 7 days intervals during the storage period.

Film properties were measured with individually prepared films in triplicate as the replicated experimental units. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the significance of each mean property value was determined (p < 0.05) with Duncan’s multiple range test using the SPSS statistical analysis computer program for Windows (SPSS Inc., USA).

The apparent photo images, surface, and cross-sectional FE-SEM images of the Chi/Gel-based films are shown in Fig. 1. The surface morphology of the neat Chi/Gel film shows a smooth, continuous structure without cracks or defects, indicating the excellent compatibility between the two biopolymers. The CDs were uniformly distributed in the Chi/Gel polymer matrix regardless of the concentration, and there was no significant change in the surface morphology. The continuous internal structure of composite films was also demonstrated in cross-sectional images, essential for composite film characterization (You et al., 2016). In general, the microstructure of composite films is mainly influenced by the compatibility between the filler molecules and the polymer matrix and the distribution of fillers within the polymer matrix. The compatibility between the polymer and CDs is presumably due to the hydrophilic property of CDs.

The molecular structure and functional groups of the Chi/Gel film and nanohybrid complex were investigated using FTIR spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 2a. A broad peak shown at 3277 cm−1 in all films was due to the hydroxyl stretching in chitosan and gelatin, which was merged in the same region with the N-H stretching (Roy & Rhim, 2021). The bands at 2938 and 2920 cm−1 were due to the C-H stretching vibrations in the alkane groups of the biopolymers (Yadav et al., 2020). The 1633 and 1547 cm−1 absorption bands were attributed to the CO stretching of amide I bands and N-H bending of amide II bands stretching vibration, respectively (Roy & Rhim, 2021). The absorption bands at 1454 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1 were due to the C-N and N-H stretching vibrations, while the 1240 cm−1 absorption band was due to the amide-III N-H stretching vibrations in the gelatin structure (Ezati & Rhim, 2021a). Also, the absorption bands for stretching vibrations of the C-O-C and C-O were observed at 1154 and 1030 cm−1 (Roy et al., 2020). However, the characteristic peaks of the CDs-added films were similar to those of the neat Chi/Gel films, except for a slight increase in peak intensity in the films incorporated with 2 wt% CDs. The increase in peak intensity in the 2 wt% CDs-added film may be due to the high concentration of CDs added to the film. The spectral similarity between the CDs-added films and the neat Chi/Gel film can be attributed to the fact that the chemical structure of the biopolymer film is not affected by the CDs addition, suggesting high compatibility between the CDs and the polymer matrix (Agarwal et al., 2020).

XRD analysis was performed to observe the effect of CDs on the crystal phase of the polymer matrix, and the results are shown in Fig. 2b. The XRD patterns of Chi/Gel films showed characteristic refraction peaks of chitosan with broadband around 2Ɵ = 13º and refraction peaks at 2Ɵ = 8.4, 18.1, and 24.0º, indicating the semi-crystalline properties of the chitosan polymer (Roy et al., 2020).

On the other hand, the peaks at about 2Ɵ = 8.4° and 22° are attributed to the diffraction peaks of gelatin. When CDs were added, there was a change in the refractive peak at 8.4° of the Chi/Gel film. When 1 wt% CD was added, the intensity of the peak was weakened, and when 2 wt% CD was added, the peak disappeared. Thus, the presence of CDs did not change the crystal structure of the base matrix and could be evidence of the coexistence of the two phases of polymer matrix and CDs (Kanimozhi et al., 2016).

The optical properties of biopolymer packaging films are often changed by adding active materials used to impart functional properties. Appearance color and optical properties are important quality attributes of packaging films that attract consumers and protect food products from photodegradation. The surface color and light transmittance of the Chi/Gel-based films are shown in Table 1. The neat Chi/Gel film has high transparency to visible light, somewhat low transmittance to UV light with a slight yellow tint, having a high L-value (brightness) with high light transmittance (90.9% of T660) and low a-value (greenness/redness). The addition of CDs significantly reduced the Chi/Gel film's brightness while increasing the redness (a-value) and blueness (b-value). As a result, the film's total color difference (ΔE) increased significantly, mainly due to the brown color of the CDs, which increased the redness and yellowness in the CDs-added films. In addition, a concentration-dependent change in color properties was observed with the addition of CDs, which is also evident in the apparent photographic image of the film (Fig. 1).

The UV barrier and transparency properties of the film were evaluated by determining the light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660), respectively, and the results are shown in Table 1. The neat Chi/Gel film has high transparency but has relatively low UV transmittance due to gelatin's inherent UV absorbing properties. The addition of CDs significantly reduced the T280 and T660 of the Chi/Gel film. The addition of CDs significantly increased the UV blocking properties of the Chi/Gel films. When 1 wt% CD was added to the Chi/Gel film, T660 decreased by less than 1%, whereas T280 decreased by 82.5%. The UV blocking properties of CDs-added films are attributed to CDs' UV light absorption properties, which show a maximum light absorption at 295 nm (Fig. S3a, Supporting Information). Light, especially ultraviolet light, promotes the breakdown and oxidation of nutrients in food through the formation of various chemicals, leading to off-flavor, rancidity, discoloration, and toxic compounds (Akbari et al., 2015). UV protection is one of the main functions of packaging films. It is an important function of packaging films that can preserve the quality of foods easily deteriorated by photocatalytic oxidation, such as high-fat foods (Ezati & Rhim, 2021b).

The mechanical properties of the packaging material are also of paramount importance, so packaging films must meet adequate mechanical strength and integrity to protect food products during storage and distribution by preventing breakage or leakage (Ezati & Rhim, 2021a). The mechanical properties of the Chi/Gel-based films are shown in Table 2. The thickness of the film was not significantly changed by the addition of CDs, which may be due to the uniform distribution of low concentration of CDs with small size in the polymer matrix as observed in the FE-SEM images.

Tensile properties (TS) of Chi/Gel films indicate that Chi/Gel films are relatively strong (TS of 75–80 MPa) with low flexibility (EB about 7.3%) and stiffness (EM about 2.8 GPa) (Roy & Rhim, 2020b, 2020a). The addition of CDs slightly increased the TS and reduced EM of the Chi/Gel film, but the difference was not statistically significant (p > 0.05). Tensile properties of the Chi/Gel film were not affected by adding CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: The WVP of composite films is usually affected by the type and interaction of the filler and polymer matrix. A series of physical processes, such as water vapor adsorption on the film surface, diffusion of water molecules through the film, and water vaporization on the other side of the film, are involved in water vapor transmission in the film (Ezati & Rhim, 2021a; Yadav et al., 2020). The WVP of the Chi/Gel-based films is shown in Fig. 3. The WVP of the neat Chi/Gel was 0.54 × 10−9 g m/m2 Pa s, which is in good agreement with the previously reported value of Chi/Gel-based binary films (Roy & Rhim, 2021). The WVP of the film was significantly decreased (p < 0.05) when CDs were added at a low concentration (1 wt%) but slightly increased when CDs were added at a higher concentration (2 wt%), probably due to the hydrophilic nature of CDs. Packaging films with low WVP values are desirable for maintaining food quality and extending the shelf life of packaged foods as their quality changes due to moisture transfer between the food and the surrounding environment during storage and distribution (Yadav et al., 2020).

The surface wettability or hydrophilicity of the Chi/Gel-based films was evaluated by measuring water contact angle (WCA). Usually, a biopolymer-based film with a WCA of less than 65° is considered to have a hydrophilic surface (Ezati et al., 2020). The WCA of the neat Chi/Gel film was 53.6° (Fig. 3), indicating that the Chi/Gel film has a hydrophilic surface. The addition of CDs slightly decreased the WCA of the Chi/Gel film, and the decrease was dependent on the CDs concentration, but the difference was not statistically significant (p > 0.05). The reduced WCA (i.e., increased hydrophilicity) of the CDs-added films was mainly due to the hydrophilicity of the CDs.

The antioxidant activity of the CDs and Chi/Gel-based films was evaluated using ABTS and DPPH methods. ABTS and DPPH contain long-lasting free radicals that change color when interacting with antioxidants, from dark green and purple to colorless or yellow/colorless. The CDs' concentration-dependent ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging activity is shown in Fig. 4a. The CDs showed significant antioxidant activity within minutes of incubation in both methods. The UV–vis spectra for CDs-added solutions showed a significant decrease in absorbance with increasing CDs concentration compared to the control solution. The characteristic ABTS and DPPH absorption bands at 734 nm and 517 nm gradually disappeared with increasing CD concentration. At a concentration of 50 and 100 μg/mL of CDs, the antioxidant activity against ABTS and DPPH was ~95%. The superior antioxidant activity of CDs may be due to its surface hydroxyl groups participating in free radical scavenging to generate more stable products in the ABTS and DPPH assays (Zhang et al., 2018). The free radicals ABTS• and DPPH• can be reduced and quenched by the H• transfer from the hydroxyl groups of the CDs to form ABTS-H and DPPH-H (Marković et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2022).

Oxidative degradation of food is a major challenge in the food industry, so it is recommended to use functional packaging films with antioxidant properties to prevent the oxidation of food, especially high-fat food (Ezati, Bang, et al., 2021; Ezati, Priyadarshi, et al., 2021). The antioxidant activity of the neat Chi/Gel film was 16.1% and 2.8% for ABTS and DPPH scavenging efficacy (Fig. 4b). However, when 1 wt% of CDs were added, the ABTS scavenging activity of the film increased dramatically to almost 94.2%, and the DPPH scavenging ability increased to ~65.4%. In addition, due to the excellent antioxidant activity of CDs, the antioxidant activity of the film was increased to 98.1% and 74.7% in the ABTS and DPPH assays by the addition of 2 wt% of CDs (Chi/Gel/CD2 film). The CD-added film's antioxidant activity was higher in the ABTS method than in the DPPH method because the hydrophilicity of the Chi/Gel film caused a faster release of CDs from the ABTS aqueous solution. At the same time hydrophilic CDs interfered with the interaction with DPPH free radicals in methanol solution, consistent with the release test results showing the higher release of CDs in aqueous solution (Ezati et al., 2020). Again, due to the CDs' surface OH groups, the CDs are more dispersed in the aqueous solution of ABTS, which leads to higher activity in the ABTS method. The CDs-added films with excellent antioxidant activity are highly likely to be used as active packaging films to prevent oxidative degradation of food during storage and distribution.

Antimicrobial activity is the most widely used functional property in active food packaging applications. The antimicrobial activity of Chi/Gel-based films was evaluated using the total viable colony count and agar well diffusion methods (
Figs. 5 and 6).

The inhibitory effect of the film on microbial growth was first qualitatively assessed using the agar well diffusion method against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, A. flavus, and C. orbiculare. Fig. 5 shows the agar well diffusion test results indicating the inhibition zone. The neat Chi/Gel film did not show any inhibition zone against the test bacteria but showed an inhibition zone of 4 ± 2 and 6 ± 3 mm for A. flavus and C. orbiculare, respectively. This result may be due to the limited penetration of the polymer into the agar and reduced diffusion and mobility in closed structures. The antifungal activity of the neat Chi/Gel film was due to the antimicrobial activity of chitosan.

On the other hand, CDs-added films showed an inhibition zone against the test microbes depending on the CDs concentration. For bacteria (L. monocytogenes and E. coli), the Chi/Gel/CD1 film showed inhibition zones of 13 ± 5 mm and 10 ± 1 mm, respectively, increased to 21 ± 3 mm and 19 ± 3 mm when tested with Chi/Gel/CD2 film. Similarly, Chi/Gel/CD2 film showed higher activity against A. flavus and C. orbiculare with an inhibition zone of 35 ± 5 mm and 36 ± 6 mm, respectively. As a result, the addition of CDs greatly improved the antimicrobial effect of Chi/Gel film and provided particularly excellent antifungal activity.

Next, the antimicrobial activity of the CDs-added film was evaluated using the total viable colony count method against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, A. flavus, and C. orbiculare, and the results are shown in Fig. 6. The neat Chi/Gel film showed some antimicrobial activity, showing a microstatic effect against the test microbes after 12 h and 3 days of incubation for bacteria and fungi, respectively, due to the antimicrobial activity of chitosan. The antimicrobial properties of chitosan are believed to inhibit bacterial growth by binding the polycationic chitosan with negatively charged bacterial cell membranes (Bonilla et al., 2014; Moradi et al., 2019; Yadav et al., 2020).

On the other hand, the CDs-added films significantly increased antimicrobial activity against test bacteria and fungi, dependent on the CDs concentration. The Chi/Gel/CD2 film completely stopped the growth of the test microorganisms after 3 h and 1 day of exposure to bacteria and fungi, respectively. And the Chi/Gel/CD1 stopped the growth of bacteria and fungi after 6 h and 1 day of exposure, respectively. The results indicated that the CDs-added film has a stronger antibacterial activity against fungi. The increased antimicrobial activity of the CDs-added film is due to the strong antimicrobial activity of the CDs (Fig. S4).

To confirm the antimicrobial activity of Chi/Gel-based films, SEM images of L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and A. flavus were acquired before and after each film treatment ( Fig. 7a). The Chi/Gel film-treated groups showed some damage to the cell surface, confirming the antimicrobial activity of chitosan. The SEM images clearly showed that microbial cells were lysed when exposed to Chi/Gel-CDs films depending on the concentration. Cell membranes collapsed and distorted shape, leaking intracellular substances, which may be attributed to the formation of ROS leading to the breakdown of the microbial cell wall, resulting in the degradation of cellular components.

To test the antimicrobial action of the films, the ROS generating capacity of the Chi/Gel-based films was measured by ORAC (oxygen radical absorbance capability) method (Fig. 7b). The control fluorescein solution did not yield any ROS within 1 h. However, the fluorescence peak intensity of the solution containing the CDs-added films gradually decreased with time, confirming the enhanced ROS generation by the CDs. The Chi/Gel, Chi/Gel/CD1, and Chi/Gel/CD2 films generated ROS of 0.6, 3.0, and 4.2 mM, respectively. The ROS generating capacity of the films confirmed the antimicrobial activity of the CDs-added films depending on the concentration.

To confirm the biocompatibility of the film, in vitro cytotoxicity test was performed using the MTT assay for 72 h on the L929 mouse fibroblast line ( Fig. 8a). Also, optical micrographs were used to see live cells in the control sample compared to the sample exposed to the Chi/Gel/based films (Fig. 8b). The neat Chi/Gel film and CDs-added films had no detrimental effect on the growth and proliferation of the fibroblasts after 72 h exposure. As expected, the cytotoxicity of the neat Chi/Gel film was negligible, i.e., the cell viability treated with the Chi/Gel film was more than 99%. The cell viability reduced slightly to 97% and 95% when tested with the Chi/Gel/CD1 and Chi/Gel/CD2 films, but the difference from the control group is not statistically significant (p > 0.05). Overall, the CDs-added Chi/Gel film showed negligible cytotoxicity against the mouse fibroblast L929 cell line, indicating safety and biocompatibility. The excellent biocompatibility of the film thus suggests that they can be safely used for food packaging and biomedical applications.

Avocado fruits were coated with Chi/Gel-based film solutions to test the effect of the coating on extending the fruit's shelf life, and the results are shown in Fig. 9. All fruits initially had a fresh appearance, and uncoated fruits had significant mold growth on the surface after 7 days of storage and more severe after 14 and 21 days of storage. However, the fruit coated with the Chi/Gel-based films showed no signs of mold growth on the surface of the fruits even after storage for 7 days. However, after 14 days of storage, mold growth started to appear on the surface of the fruits coated with the neat Chi/Gel film. The delayed growth of mold of the fruit coated with the neat Chi/Gel film was due to the antifungal activity of chitosan, as observed in the microbial test of the film (Fig. 9). However, mold growth was not observed on the fruits coated with the CDs-added films until the 14th day of storage. The mold growth was observed on the fruits coated with the Chi/Gel/CD1 film on the 21st day of storage, but the fruits coated with the Chi/Gel/CD2 film did not show any sign of mold growth even after 21 days of storage. Overall, the CDs-added films showed significant antifungal activity in the avocado packaging test, and the antifungal activity depended on the CDs concentration. The coating effect of avocados is due to the antimicrobial properties of CDs-added Chi/Gel-based films. The storage test results of avocados are in good agreement with the antimicrobial test results of the films. The CDs-added Chi/Gel films will likely have an active coating on the fruit to increase shelf life and maintain the quality during post-harvest distribution.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using glucose as a carbon source by a hydrothermal method. The CDs showed strong antimicrobial and antioxidant activity. The addition of CDs did not affect the mechanical properties, water vapor permeability, and water contact angle of the films while significantly increasing the UV blocking properties of Chi/Gel-based films. The CDs-added films showed negligible cytotoxicity to L929 mouse fibroblasts, suggesting their use in food packaging applications. The CDs-added film solution was used for avocado coating inhibited mold growth on the fruit surface and extended the fruit shelf life by more than 14 days. Hence, the Chi/Gel/CDs coating formulations have a high potential for safe packaging of functional materials to extend the shelf life of fruits after harvest.

Parya Ezati: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data analysis, Writing – original draft. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data analysis, Writing – original draft. Zeinab Rezaei: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no known competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. 2022R1A2B02001422) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental section: Carbon dots characterization and film characterization and properties.

Results: Carbon dots characterizations, fluorescence intensity measurement (Fig. S1), TEM, XPS, and XRD (Fig. S2 and Table S1), light absorption characteristics (Fig. S3), and antimicrobial activity of carbon dots (Fig. S4).
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.fpsl.2022.100878.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
